pathophysiology_summary: Melasma is acquired symmetrical facial hyperpigmentation from UV- and hormone-driven (here a recently started combined OCP) melanocyte activation, producing smooth, poorly-defined light-to-dark brown macules/patches on the malar cheeks, forehead and upper lip, with normal skin texture (no scaling, induration or ulceration). A weekend of UV exposure caused a dramatic darkening, catastrophised by the patient as a 'spreading skin cancer', triggering a panic reaction. Discriminators: the completely symmetrical, bilateral, smooth macular pattern without a single asymmetric/irregular/raised/changing lesion argues against lentigo maligna/melanoma; normal blood pressure, no systemic weakness/vomiting and no palmar-crease/mucosal hyperpigmentation argue against Addison's disease; and the absence of a preceding inflammatory dermatosis in the same areas argues against post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation. Management is reassurance, rigorous daily broad-spectrum sun protection (the mainstay), avoiding skin irritation, discussing OCP review, and considering topical lightening agents (e.g., hydroquinone) electively — plus support for the acute anxiety, with a skin-check safety-net for any future single atypical lesion.

differential.evidence_basis: Symmetrical smooth facial brown patches, UV/OCP triggers.
Screen for any single asymmetric/irregular/raised/changing pigmented lesion.
Screen for systemic hyperpigmentation and adrenal-insufficiency features.
No preceding inflammatory dermatosis in the same areas.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Reassure only AFTER screening for a single atypical lesion (melanoma) and systemic hyperpigmentation (Addison's); manage the acute anxiety.

differential.evidence_basis: Symmetrical smooth benign pattern; no atypical lesion or systemic features.
No single asymmetric/irregular/changing lesion — remains a routine skin-check safety-net.
Working diagnosis: reassurance + sun protection + OCP review + anxiety support; elective lightening therapy; skin-check safety-net.

red_flags.description: A single asymmetric/irregular/raised/changing pigmented lesion (possible melanoma/lentigo maligna)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would need dermoscopy/biopsy; melasma is symmetrical and macular, unlike melanoma.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A coincidental melanoma could be overlooked amid reassurance.

red_flags.description: Systemic hyperpigmentation with adrenal-insufficiency features (weakness, dizziness, vomiting, low BP, palmar/mucosal pigmentation)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Addison's disease can present with hyperpigmentation and is life-threatening in crisis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reassuring benign melasma without excluding a single atypical lesion or Addison's.
Sending a clearly benign symmetrical melasma to ED for 'skin cancer'.
Recommending harsh scrubs/peels that can darken melasma.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassure after screening; advise strict daily sun protection, avoid irritation, discuss OCP review and elective lightening therapy, and support the anxiety; routine skin check and safety-net for any single changing lesion or systemic features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring without a melanoma/Addison's screen, or over-escalating benign melasma.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Melasma: rigorous broad-spectrum sun protection is the mainstay; consider topical lightening agents; review hormonal triggers (OCP); avoid skin irritation.
